title: Model Deployment
summary: Move from Foundry model catalog discovery to a managed-identity-created, callable deployment endpoint while choosing deployment type, region, quota, version policy, lifecycle strategy, and cost model.

concepts.title: Model catalog, providers, and collections
concepts.firstPrinciples: A model cannot be deployed safely until you know it exists, who provides it, what terms apply, where it can run, and which deployment types it supports.
concepts.definition: The Microsoft Foundry model catalog is the searchable inventory of foundation, reasoning, small language, multimodal, industry, and partner models. It includes collections and providers such as Azure OpenAI, Microsoft Phi, Meta Llama, Mistral, Cohere, DeepSeek, NVIDIA, Hugging Face, and more. Models are broadly Foundry Models sold by Azure or Models from partners and community.
concepts.analogy: The catalog is an airport departure board. A model card is the flight detail page: provider, destination capabilities, regions, terms, benchmark clues, and boarding options.
concepts.mentalModel: Browse Discover then Models, filter by collection, capability, inference task, deployment option, and region. Open the model card, then record provider, exact model name, model version, legal terms, supported regions, and deployment types before creating anything.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
  Learner[learner] --> Catalog[model catalog]
  Catalog --> Filters[filters]
  Filters --> Card[model card]
  Card --> Options[deployment options]
  Options --> Endpoint[endpoint]

code.caption: Portal discovery checklist; no secrets or keys.
code.content:
# Open the Foundry portal and discover a deployable model.
open "https://ai.azure.com"

# In the portal:
# 1. Select Discover, then Models.
# 2. Filter by collection, capability, inference task, deployment option, and region.
# 3. Open the model card.
# 4. Record provider, model name, model version, regions, deployment types, and terms.

drills.q: What does the catalog answer before deployment?
drills.a: Which models exist, who provides them, where they run, and how they can be deployed.

drills.q: Are all catalog models owned by Microsoft?
drills.a: No. Some are sold by Azure; many are partner or community models with their own terms.

drills.q: Why inspect the model card?
drills.a: It contains deployment options, version data, license or terms, benchmarks, and region information.

concepts.teachBack: The catalog is the map from idea to deployable model. I use filters to narrow the model list and the model card to verify provider, terms, region, version, and deployment type. I do not deploy until those checks match my workload.

glossary.definition: The organization or product family supplying the model.
A catalog grouping, often by provider or model family.
The catalog detail page for a model.
A model supplied by an external provider or community source.

sources.title: Microsoft Foundry Models overview
Deploy Microsoft Foundry Models in the Foundry portal

concepts.title: Standard, Global, Data Zone, and Provisioned choices
concepts.firstPrinciples: Every endpoint has to answer three physical questions: where inference processing may happen, how capacity is allocated, and how you pay.
concepts.definition: Current Foundry deployment types include Global Standard, Data Zone Standard, Standard regional, Global Provisioned, Data Zone Provisioned, Regional Provisioned, Batch variants, Developer, and Instant preview. Standard-family deployments are pay-per-token. Provisioned-family deployments reserve capacity with PTUs. Global can process in any Azure region, Data Zone processes within a Microsoft-defined US or EU zone, and Standard regional processes in the deployment region.
concepts.analogy: Deployment type is shipping class. Global is a worldwide network, Data Zone is a restricted continent pass, regional is local delivery, and provisioned is reserving a dedicated truck.
concepts.mentalModel: Start with compliance boundary, then traffic pattern. Use Global Standard for broad availability and high default quota, Data Zone Standard for US or EU zone processing, Standard for single-region processing, and Provisioned when predictable high throughput and lower latency variance justify reserved capacity.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart TD
  Need[workload need] --> Residency[data boundary]
  Need --> Traffic[traffic pattern]
  Residency --> Global[Global Standard]
  Residency --> Zone[Data Zone Standard]
  Residency --> Regional[Standard]
  Traffic --> Paygo[pay per token]
  Traffic --> PTU[reserved PTU]

code.caption: Current deployment type SKU names used in control-plane commands.
code.content:
# Pay-as-you-go deployment type SKU names.
SKU_GLOBAL="GlobalStandard"
SKU_DATA_ZONE="DataZoneStandard"
SKU_REGIONAL="Standard"

# Provisioned deployment type SKU names.
SKU_GLOBAL_PTU="GlobalProvisionedManaged"
SKU_DATA_ZONE_PTU="DataZoneProvisionedManaged"
SKU_REGIONAL_PTU="ProvisionedManaged"

echo "Check the model card before choosing: $SKU_GLOBAL, $SKU_DATA_ZONE, $SKU_REGIONAL"

drills.q: Which type usually gives broad availability?
drills.a: Global Standard.

drills.q: Which types reserve capacity?
drills.a: Global Provisioned, Data Zone Provisioned, and Regional Provisioned.

drills.q: Which type keeps processing in a US or EU zone?
drills.a: Data Zone Standard or Data Zone Provisioned.

concepts.teachBack: Deployment type is the big architectural switch. It controls processing geography, billing style, and capacity behavior. I choose it from compliance and traffic needs, not from habit.

glossary.definition: Pay-per-token deployment with global Azure routing.
Pay-per-token deployment constrained to a US or EU data zone.
Regional pay-per-token deployment.
Provisioned Throughput Unit for reserved model capacity.

sources.title: Understanding deployment types in Microsoft Foundry Models
Microsoft Foundry Models quotas and limits

concepts.title: Serverless API versus managed compute
concepts.firstPrinciples: A model can be exposed as an API without your own host, or deployed onto managed compute you size and pay for. The right choice depends on control, cost shape, and operational burden.
concepts.definition: Serverless API deployments, often described as MaaS, provide API access to a provider-hosted model and typically bill by inputs and outputs such as tokens. Managed compute, documented in Foundry classic as managed online deployment, places model weights behind a real-time managed online endpoint on dedicated VM resources and bills by compute-hours. New Foundry guidance recommends Foundry resource deployments for many Foundry Models, while classic serverless and managed compute remain important patterns to recognize.
concepts.analogy: Serverless is ordering electricity from the grid. Managed compute is leasing a generator that Azure maintains but you size and pay for while it runs.
concepts.mentalModel: Use serverless when you want minimal hosting responsibility and usage billing. Use managed compute when a model requires it or when you need VM sizing, instance count, or dedicated host control. Always check portal version, model support, region support, Marketplace terms, and expected idle cost.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
  Model[model] --> Serverless[serverless API]
  Serverless --> Provider[provider hosted]
  Serverless --> TokenBilling[token billing]
  Model --> Managed[managed compute]
  Managed --> VM[managed VM instances]
  Managed --> ComputeBilling[compute hours]

code.caption: Managed identity pattern for managed compute client setup.
code.content:
from azure.ai.ml import MLClient
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()  # Uses managed identity on Azure hosts.
ml_client = MLClient(
    credential=credential,
    subscription_id="<subscription-id>",
    resource_group_name="<resource-group>",
    workspace_name="<hub-project-or-aml-workspace>",
)

# Managed compute deployment creation uses ManagedOnlineEndpoint and
# ManagedOnlineDeployment. This lane stops at deployment setup, not inference.
for endpoint in ml_client.online_endpoints.list():
    print(endpoint.name)

drills.q: Does serverless host the model on VMs in your subscription?
drills.a: No, the provider hosts and manages the API infrastructure.

drills.q: What is the managed compute target URI shape shown in docs?
drills.a: https://<endpoint-name>.<region>.inference.ml.azure.com/score

drills.q: What is the managed compute idle-cost risk?
drills.a: You can pay for VM resources while traffic is low or zero.

concepts.teachBack: Serverless gives me a hosted API and usage billing. Managed compute gives me dedicated managed infrastructure and compute-hour billing. I choose based on model support, control needs, and cost shape.

glossary.definition: Hosted model API without customer-managed compute.
Model as a service.
Managed online endpoint backed by VM resources.
Cost based on VM size, instance count, and running time.

sources.title: Deploy models as serverless API deployments
Deploy models with managed compute
Microsoft Foundry Models overview

concepts.title: Deployment name, model name, and model version
concepts.firstPrinciples: Applications need a stable name to call, but operators need freedom to choose or migrate the backing model version. The deployment object separates those concerns.
concepts.definition: A deployment object contains a deployment name, underlying model name, model version, SKU or deployment type, capacity, state, and rate limits. The deployment name is the callable alias used to route requests. The model name identifies the provider-specific model family, while the version identifies a specific release.
concepts.analogy: The deployment name is a restaurant menu item such as house burger. The kitchen can change the ingredient batch or recipe version, but customers order by the stable menu name.
concepts.mentalModel: Name deployments as contracts, such as prod-chat-gpt4o-mini or eval-llama32-vision. Keep a record of the model name, version, deployment type, capacity, owner, and consuming applications so migration is controlled.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
  App[application] --> Alias[deployment name]
  Alias --> Model[model name]
  Model --> Version[model version]
  Alias --> SKU[deployment type]
  Alias --> Limits[rate limits]

code.caption: Inspect alias, backing model, version, and state.
code.content:
az login --identity

az cognitiveservices account deployment show   --resource-group "<resource-group>"   --name "<foundry-or-openai-account-name>"   --deployment-name "<deployment-alias>"   --query "{alias:name, sku:sku.name, model:properties.model.name, version:properties.model.version, state:properties.provisioningState}"

drills.q: Which value is the alias?
drills.a: The deployment name.

drills.q: Can one model version have multiple deployments?
drills.a: Yes, subject to quota and resource limits.

drills.q: Why not call the raw model version directly in app config?
drills.a: The deployment alias gives operators a stable routing and migration point.

concepts.teachBack: A deployment is the call target, not just the model. The deployment name is the alias my app uses. The model name and version are the backing implementation details I track for lifecycle control.

glossary.definition: The callable alias for a deployed model.
The provider-specific model family identifier.
A specific model release.
The deployment control-plane status.

sources.title: Deploy Microsoft Foundry Models in the Foundry portal
Working with Azure OpenAI model updates

concepts.title: Creating deployments with managed identity
concepts.firstPrinciples: Creating a model deployment is a control-plane action: authenticate, select the resource, choose model name and version, set deployment type and capacity, then ask Azure Resource Manager to create the child resource.
concepts.definition: You can create deployments in the portal, with Azure CLI, or with infrastructure as code such as Bicep. In automation, use managed identity only: az login --identity for CLI or DefaultAzureCredential for SDK code. For Azure OpenAI and many Foundry model deployments, az cognitiveservices account deployment create accepts deployment name, model format, model name, model version, SKU name, and capacity.
concepts.analogy: The portal is hand-placing a machine on a line. CLI and Bicep are sending a signed work order from a robot with an employee badge, not a shared password.
concepts.mentalModel: Portal path: Discover Models, select model card, Deploy, choose default or custom settings, accept Marketplace terms if needed, choose deployment type, name, and quota, then verify Succeeded. CLI path: managed identity login, create deployment, show deployment. Bicep path: declare Microsoft.CognitiveServices/accounts/deployments and apply with managed identity.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart TD
  MI[managed identity] --> RBAC[RBAC role]
  RBAC --> CLI[az deployment create]
  RBAC --> Bicep[Bicep apply]
  CLI --> Deployment[model deployment]
  Bicep --> Deployment

code.caption: Runnable CLI create path using managed identity only.
code.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Run on an Azure host with a managed identity granted deployment permissions.
az login --identity

RESOURCE_GROUP="<resource-group>"
ACCOUNT_NAME="<foundry-or-openai-account-name>"
DEPLOYMENT_NAME="prod-gpt-4o-mini"
MODEL_NAME="gpt-4o-mini"
MODEL_VERSION="<model-version-from-model-card>"
SKU_NAME="GlobalStandard"   # Or DataZoneStandard or Standard if supported.
CAPACITY="10"              # Capacity maps to quota and rate limits for the model/SKU.

az cognitiveservices account deployment create   --resource-group "$RESOURCE_GROUP"   --name "$ACCOUNT_NAME"   --deployment-name "$DEPLOYMENT_NAME"   --model-format "OpenAI"   --model-name "$MODEL_NAME"   --model-version "$MODEL_VERSION"   --sku-name "$SKU_NAME"   --sku-capacity "$CAPACITY"

az cognitiveservices account deployment show   --resource-group "$RESOURCE_GROUP"   --name "$ACCOUNT_NAME"   --deployment-name "$DEPLOYMENT_NAME"   --query "{name:name, sku:sku, model:properties.model, state:properties.provisioningState}"

drills.q: Why use az login --identity?
drills.a: It authenticates with managed identity and avoids keys, secrets, and service principals.

drills.q: What is SKU_NAME?
drills.a: The deployment type code, such as GlobalStandard.

drills.q: What role is needed?
drills.a: A role with permission to create and manage deployments, such as Cognitive Services Contributor or equivalent.

concepts.teachBack: Deployment creation is an Azure management operation. The managed identity supplies authentication and RBAC supplies authorization. The model name, version, deployment alias, SKU, and capacity define what Azure creates.

glossary.definition: An Azure-managed Microsoft Entra identity assigned to a resource.
Role-based access control.
The code for deployment type or capacity class.
Azure infrastructure as code language.

sources.title: Deploy Microsoft Foundry Models in the Foundry portal
Understanding deployment types in Microsoft Foundry Models
Manage Azure OpenAI quota

concepts.title: Endpoints and target URIs
concepts.firstPrinciples: A deployment is only callable when it is reachable through a network address and route. Endpoint, deployment name, and model version are different layers.
concepts.definition: AIProjectClient uses a Foundry project endpoint shaped like https://{ai-services-account-name}.services.ai.azure.com/api/projects/{project-name}. Managed compute target URIs in classic managed online deployments are shaped like https://<endpoint-name>.<region>.inference.ml.azure.com/score. Portal deployment detail pages show endpoint details, code, consume, or target URI depending on deployment type.
concepts.analogy: The endpoint is the building address, the deployment name is the room number, and the model version is the machine inside the room.
concepts.mentalModel: After deployment, record the project or resource endpoint, deployment alias, target URI if applicable, deployment type, and model version. Do not confuse endpoint URL with model name. The endpoint gets traffic to the resource; the deployment alias routes it to capacity.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
  Client[client] --> Endpoint[project or resource endpoint]
  Endpoint --> Alias[deployment alias]
  Alias --> Model[model version]
  Managed[managed online endpoint] --> Target[target URI]

code.caption: Construct AIProjectClient with managed identity and list deployment names.
code.content:
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

project_endpoint = "https://<account>.services.ai.azure.com/api/projects/<project>"
client = AIProjectClient(endpoint=project_endpoint, credential=DefaultAzureCredential())

# Inventory only. Inference call details are covered by another lane.
for deployment in client.deployments.list():
    print(deployment.get("name"), deployment.get("type"))

drills.q: Is an endpoint the same as a deployment name?
drills.a: No. The endpoint is the address; the deployment name is the route or alias.

drills.q: Where do you find endpoint details?
drills.a: In Foundry deployment details, code, consume, or model pages depending on deployment type.

drills.q: What does AIProjectClient need?
drills.a: A project endpoint and TokenCredential such as DefaultAzureCredential.

concepts.teachBack: Endpoint, alias, and version are separate. I call the endpoint and route to the deployment alias. I track the backing model version for operations and lifecycle management.

glossary.definition: The Foundry project URL used by AIProjectClient.
The scoring URL for a managed online endpoint.
The mapping from request to deployment alias.
Azure SDK credential interface used by DefaultAzureCredential.

sources.title: AIProjectClient class
Deploy models with managed compute
Deploy Microsoft Foundry Models in the Foundry portal

concepts.title: Capacity, quota, TPM, PTU, and rate limits
concepts.firstPrinciples: AI capacity is finite. Azure turns capacity into quota allocations and rate limits so one deployment cannot consume unlimited shared infrastructure.
concepts.definition: Quota is allocated by subscription and commonly by model, region, deployment type, or newer shared pools depending on model rollout. Pay-as-you-go deployments allocate Tokens per Minute to deployments; Requests per Minute is also enforced. Provisioned deployments use PTUs to reserve throughput. Runtime throttling commonly appears as HTTP 429 when token or request limits are exceeded.
concepts.analogy: Quota is your water tank. TPM is the pipe flow rate. PTU is a reserved pipe. A 429 is the valve saying the flow is too high.
concepts.mentalModel: Before deployment, check quota for model, region, version, and deployment type. Allocate only needed TPM. If exhausted, reduce allocation elsewhere, choose another region or deployment type, or request quota. For production, monitor remaining request and token headers and implement retries in the inference layer.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart TD
  Subscription[subscription quota] --> Pool[model region deployment type pool]
  Pool --> A[deployment A TPM]
  Pool --> B[deployment B TPM]
  PTU[PTU reservation] --> Predictable[predictable throughput]
  A --> Limit[429 if exceeded]

code.caption: Inspect SKU and rate-limit data for a deployment.
code.content:
az login --identity

az cognitiveservices account deployment show   --resource-group "<resource-group>"   --name "<foundry-or-openai-account-name>"   --deployment-name "<deployment-name>"   --query "{sku:sku, rateLimits:properties.rateLimits, model:properties.model}"

# If quota is exhausted, request more quota or reallocate from another deployment.
# Microsoft Learn links the quota request form: https://aka.ms/oai/stuquotarequest

drills.q: What is TPM?
drills.a: Tokens per minute, used for quota and rate-limit allocation.

drills.q: What is PTU?
drills.a: Provisioned Throughput Unit, reserved model processing capacity.

drills.q: What usually causes 429?
drills.a: A request or token rate limit was exceeded.

concepts.teachBack: Quota is planned before deployment and felt during runtime. TPM controls pay-as-you-go throughput, while PTUs reserve capacity. Rate limits are deployment-level guardrails that require monitoring and retries.

glossary.definition: Tokens per minute.
Requests per minute.
Provisioned Throughput Unit.
HTTP Too Many Requests throttling response.

sources.title: Manage Azure OpenAI quota
Microsoft Foundry Models quotas and limits
Understanding deployment types in Microsoft Foundry Models

concepts.title: Version pinning, auto-update, and retirement
concepts.firstPrinciples: Models are living software. A deployment that works today can drift through updates or stop at retirement unless you choose version policy and migration strategy deliberately.
concepts.definition: Foundry lifecycle stages include Preview, Generally Available, Legacy, Deprecated, and Retired. Azure OpenAI deployment version policies include OnceNewDefaultVersionAvailable, OnceCurrentVersionExpired, and NoAutoUpgrade where supported. Pinning a specific model version improves repeatability, but retirement still requires migration. Retired models no longer serve inference and docs describe 410 Gone.
concepts.analogy: A model version is a textbook edition. Pinning means teaching from edition 3; retirement means edition 3 leaves the bookstore and you must migrate to edition 4.
concepts.mentalModel: For production, pin versions or use conservative upgrade policy, track retirement schedules, deploy replacement versions in parallel, test quality and latency, then cut over application configuration or alias use. For experiments, auto-update to default can be acceptable.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
  Preview --> GA
  GA --> Legacy
  Legacy --> Deprecated
  Deprecated --> Retired
  Old[old deployment] --> New[test replacement]
  New --> Cutover[planned cutover]

code.caption: Read model version and version upgrade option.
code.content:
az login --identity

az cognitiveservices account deployment show   --resource-group "<resource-group>"   --name "<foundry-or-openai-account-name>"   --deployment-name "<deployment-name>"   --query "{model:properties.model, versionUpgradeOption:properties.versionUpgradeOption, state:properties.provisioningState}"

# Microsoft Learn notes Azure CLI can read versionUpgradeOption; update support may require ARM or other supported tooling.

drills.q: Why pin a model version?
drills.a: To preserve behavior while you validate future versions.

drills.q: What happens at retirement?
drills.a: The model no longer serves; requests can return 410 Gone.

drills.q: What is the safest migration pattern?
drills.a: Deploy a replacement in parallel, test it, then cut over intentionally.

concepts.teachBack: Version policy is production hygiene. I choose whether to pin or auto-update, then track retirement. Migration is a planned parallel deployment and cutover, not an emergency.

glossary.definition: Experimental lifecycle stage.
Generally Available production stage.
Restricted stage before retirement.
No longer serving inference.
Policy controlling supported automatic version updates.

sources.title: Foundry Models lifecycle and support policy
Working with Azure OpenAI model updates

concepts.title: Region and data residency
concepts.firstPrinciples: Prompts and responses are processed somewhere. Data residency requirements decide which deployment types and regions are even allowed before cost or latency optimization begins.
concepts.definition: Foundry docs state data stored at rest remains in the designated Azure geography, while inference processing depends on deployment type: Global may process in any Azure region, DataZone processes only within the Microsoft-specified US or EU zone, and Standard regional processes in the deployment region. Model availability and quota vary by region, version, and deployment type.
concepts.analogy: Data residency is a travel permission slip. Global can travel widely, Data Zone can travel inside a defined US or EU area, and regional must stay in one city.
concepts.mentalModel: Choose compliance boundary first, then verify the model, version, deployment type, region, and quota. If unavailable, change model, deployment type, or region; do not silently relax residency requirements.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart TD
  Requirement[residency requirement] --> Global[Any Azure region]
  Requirement --> Zone[US or EU data zone]
  Requirement --> Regional[one Azure region]
  Zone --> Availability[model availability]
  Regional --> Quota[regional quota]

code.caption: Region planning variables to fill before deployment.
code.content:
COMPLIANCE_BOUNDARY="EU data zone"       # global allowed, US data zone, EU data zone, or single region
PREFERRED_REGION="swedencentral"         # Must support model and deployment type
DEPLOYMENT_TYPE="DataZoneStandard"       # Must match compliance boundary
MODEL_NAME="<model-name>"
MODEL_VERSION="<model-version>"

echo "Plan: $MODEL_NAME $MODEL_VERSION as $DEPLOYMENT_TYPE in $PREFERRED_REGION for $COMPLIANCE_BOUNDARY"

drills.q: Can Global Standard process outside the resource region?
drills.a: Yes, global types may process in any Azure region.

drills.q: Which types are for US or EU zone processing?
drills.a: DataZone deployment types.

drills.q: What if a required model is unavailable in region?
drills.a: Choose another supported region, deployment type, or model, or request capacity where supported.

concepts.teachBack: Region is an architecture decision, not a cosmetic dropdown. Deployment type defines where inference can be processed. I verify model availability and quota inside the required boundary.

glossary.definition: Stored data retained in an Azure geography.
Where prompts and responses are processed during requests.
Microsoft-defined US or EU processing boundary.
Deployment whose processing stays in the selected region.

sources.title: Understanding deployment types in Microsoft Foundry Models
Microsoft Foundry Models quotas and limits
Foundry Models lifecycle and support policy

concepts.title: List, inspect, and delete deployments
concepts.firstPrinciples: Deployments are inventory and cost-bearing resources. You need to know what exists, inspect its backing model, and delete stale deployments without guessing.
concepts.definition: The current stable azure.ai.projects AIProjectClient exposes client.deployments. Microsoft Learn documents DeploymentsOperations.list and DeploymentsOperations.get; list can filter by model publisher, model name, and deployment type. The same stable page does not document delete, so deletion should use the portal, Azure Resource Manager, or Azure CLI for account deployments unless a future SDK version adds delete.
concepts.analogy: The SDK is the clipboard for inventory. The portal or management CLI is the loading dock for removal when the clipboard has no delete button.
concepts.mentalModel: Use AIProjectClient.deployments.list for project inventory and get for one deployment. Use az cognitiveservices account deployment delete or portal cleanup where supported. Confirm the alias and resource before deleting because deletion removes a callable route and can free quota or stop apps.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart LR
  SDK[AIProjectClient.deployments] --> List[list]
  SDK --> Get[get]
  Delete[delete] --> CLI[az or portal]
  CLI --> Removed[removed deployment]

code.caption: List and inspect deployments with AIProjectClient using managed identity.
code.content:
from azure.ai.projects import AIProjectClient
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

client = AIProjectClient(
    endpoint="https://<account>.services.ai.azure.com/api/projects/<project>",
    credential=DefaultAzureCredential(),
)

for deployment in client.deployments.list():
    print(deployment.get("name"), deployment.get("type"), deployment.get("model_name"))

one = client.deployments.get("<deployment-name>")
print(one)

# Stable AIProjectClient.deployments docs currently show list and get, not delete.
# For deletion, use portal, ARM, or Azure CLI where supported.

drills.q: What operations are documented on stable AIProjectClient.deployments?
drills.a: list and get.

drills.q: Is delete documented on stable AIProjectClient.deployments?
drills.a: No.

drills.q: How can deletion be performed today?
drills.a: Use portal, Azure Resource Manager, or Azure CLI such as az cognitiveservices account deployment delete when applicable.

concepts.teachBack: AIProjectClient gives me deployment inventory and inspection. Current stable docs show list and get only. For deletion, I use a supported management path and verify the alias before removal.

glossary.definition: AIProjectClient sub-client for deployment inventory.
Return deployed models in a project.
Retrieve a deployment by name.
Cleanup operation not shown on current stable AIProjectClient.deployments docs.

sources.title: AIProjectClient class
DeploymentsOperations class
Deploy Microsoft Foundry Models in the Foundry portal

concepts.title: Cost mental model
concepts.firstPrinciples: Cost follows the scarce thing you consume or reserve: tokens, throughput, or machines. A good deployment plan chooses the cost shape before optimizing exact prices.
concepts.definition: Standard, Global Standard, Data Zone Standard, and serverless-style deployments commonly use pay-per-token or usage billing. Provisioned deployment types charge for reserved PTU capacity. Managed compute charges for VM resources and time, so idle endpoints can still cost money. Exact prices change; the durable mental model is per-token versus reserved throughput versus compute-hours.
concepts.analogy: Pay-per-token is paying per taxi ride. PTU is a monthly reserved lane. Managed compute is leasing the vehicle by the hour.
concepts.mentalModel: Use pay-as-you-go for prototypes and bursty traffic, provisioned for steady high-throughput workloads needing lower latency variance, and managed compute when host control or model support requires it. Watch overallocated TPM, unused PTUs, and idle managed compute.
concepts.diagram:
flowchart TD
  Workload[workload] --> Bursty[bursty]
  Workload --> Steady[steady high volume]
  Workload --> Custom[custom host]
  Bursty --> Tokens[per token]
  Steady --> PTUCost[reserved PTU]
  Custom --> Compute[compute hours]

code.caption: Small helper to choose the first cost bucket to estimate.

drills.q: What cost shape fits Global Standard?
drills.a: Pay-per-token usage billing.

drills.q: What cost shape fits provisioned deployments?
drills.a: Reserved PTU capacity.

drills.q: What cost shape fits managed compute?
drills.a: VM resource size, instance count, and running hours.

concepts.teachBack: Cost is a shape before it is a number. Pay-as-you-go follows usage, provisioned follows reserved capacity, and managed compute follows running machines. I pick the shape that matches workload and compliance needs.

glossary.definition: Usage billing tied to inputs and outputs such as tokens.
Capacity paid for ahead of use.
Billing based on machine resources over time.
Cost incurred when resources run with little traffic.

quiz.q: What should you verify first before deploying a catalog model?
options: Whether the endpoint name is short
quiz.explain: Unsupported regions or deployment types block deployment or violate requirements.

quiz.q: What does a deployment name usually represent?
options: A callable alias for a deployed model
quiz.explain: Applications route to the deployment alias, which points to model details.

quiz.q: Which deployment type family reserves throughput?
quiz.explain: Provisioned deployments reserve throughput with PTUs.

quiz.q: Which authentication is allowed in examples here?
options: Managed identity through DefaultAzureCredential or az login --identity
quiz.explain: The lane requires managed identity only.

quiz.q: What does current stable AIProjectClient.deployments document?
quiz.explain: The stable DeploymentsOperations docs list list and get.

quiz.q: What can a retired model return when it no longer serves inference?
quiz.explain: The lifecycle docs describe retired models as no longer serving inference with 410 Gone.

quiz.q: What cost shape fits managed compute?
options: VM size times instance count times hours
quiz.explain: Managed compute bills by compute resources over time.

quiz.q: Which deployment type keeps inference processing within a US or EU zone?
quiz.explain: Data Zone deployment types restrict processing to the Microsoft-defined US or EU zone.
